title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with Rosemary & Olive Oil
seo.metaDescription: Master authentic Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil. This traditional Italian recipe brings the taste of Genoa to your kitchen. Perfect for any meal!
longTailKeywords: how to make Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil
seo.h1: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with Rosemary & Olive Oil
h2Headings: Ingredients for Rosemary Olive Oil Focaccia
seo.imageAlt: Freshly baked Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a slice of authentic Focaccia Genovese, a classic Italian bread infused with fragrant rosemary and rich olive oil.

description: Experience the true taste of Liguria with this authentic Focaccia Genovese, a beloved Italian classic. This recipe creates a wonderfully soft and airy bread, characterized by its signature dimples, generous drizzle of extra virgin olive oil, and fragrant rosemary. Perfect as an appetizer, side dish, or a delightful snack on its own.

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large bowl, combine the warm water and active dry yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until the mixture becomes foamy, indicating the yeast is active.

instructions.title: Mix the Dough
instructions.text: Add the all-purpose flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Mix with a sturdy spoon or your hands until a shaggy, cohesive dough forms. It will be sticky at this stage.

instructions.title: Knead and First Rise
instructions.text: Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface. Knead the dough for 8-10 minutes until it becomes smooth, elastic, and less sticky. Place the kneaded dough in a lightly greased bowl, cover it with plastic wrap or a damp cloth, and let it rise in a warm place for 1.5-2 hours, or until it has doubled in size.

instructions.title: Prepare the Pan and Dimple
instructions.text: Gently transfer the risen dough to a well-oiled 9x13 inch (23x33 cm) baking pan. Gently stretch the dough with your fingertips to fit the entire pan. Using your oiled fingertips, create deep dimples all over the surface of the dough, pushing almost to the bottom of the pan.

instructions.title: Second Rise and Topping
instructions.text: Drizzle the dimpled dough generously with extra virgin olive oil. Sprinkle evenly with the fresh rosemary leaves and flaky sea salt. Cover the pan again and let it rise for a second time for 30-45 minutes. Preheat your oven to 220°C (425°F) during this time.

instructions.title: Bake the Focaccia
instructions.text: Place the focaccia in the preheated oven and bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the top is golden brown and the crust is crisp. The internal temperature should be around 90-93°C (195-200°F).

instructions.title: Cool and Serve
instructions.text: Once baked, carefully remove the focaccia from the oven. Transfer it to a wire rack to cool slightly. For an extra touch of flavor and moisture, you can drizzle a little more olive oil over the warm focaccia before slicing and serving. Enjoy warm or at room temperature.

tips: For the best authentic flavor, always use a high-quality Italian extra virgin olive oil for both the dough and the final drizzle.
If fresh rosemary is unavailable, you can substitute with 1 teaspoon of dried rosemary, but crumble it between your fingers to release its aroma.
Focaccia is best enjoyed fresh on the day it's baked. Store any leftovers in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 2 days and reheat gently in the oven for a few minutes before serving.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the sun-drenched Ligurian coast of Italy, where simple ingredients transform into a masterpiece of texture and flavor: Focaccia Genovese. This beloved Italian flatbread, particularly when infused with aromatic rosemary and rich olive oil, is more than just a baked good; it’s a taste of tradition, a comforting embrace, and a testament to the beauty of uncomplicated, high-quality cuisine. Forget dry, dense imitations; true Focaccia Genovese is a revelation – an airy, dimpled canvas drenched in golden oil, boasting a crisp exterior and a tender, chewy crumb. If you've ever dreamt of filling your kitchen with the irresistible aroma of Italian baking, mastering this classic is a delightful place to start.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?

At its heart, Focaccia Genovese is the quintessential flatbread from Genoa, the capital of Italy's Liguria region. Unlike its Roman counterparts, which can sometimes be thicker and breadier, the Genovese style is typically thinner, lighter, and characterized by its distinctively dimpled surface. These indentations aren't just for show; they're vital pockets designed to pool with a generous amount of extra virgin olive oil and coarse sea salt, creating bursts of flavor and a wonderfully crisp texture on top. Historically, focaccia was a humble baker's treat, often baked in the residual heat of a wood-fired oven before bread, served as a morning snack or a quick meal. Today, it remains a beloved staple, cherished for its versatility and the way it perfectly embodies the Mediterranean diet's emphasis on fresh, wholesome ingredients. The magic of Focaccia Genovese lies in its apparent simplicity: flour, water, yeast, salt, and copious amounts of exquisite olive oil. It’s a dish where the quality of each ingredient shines through, creating a depth of flavor that is both rustic and refined.

## Ingredients for Rosemary Olive Oil Focaccia

Crafting the perfect rosemary olive oil Focaccia Genovese starts with selecting the right components. Each ingredient plays a crucial role in achieving that sought-after texture and taste.

*   **Flour:** While "00" flour (a finely milled Italian flour) is traditional for its delicate gluten structure, a good quality strong bread flour with a high protein content will also yield excellent results, providing the necessary elasticity for a chewy crumb.
*   **Water:** Lukewarm water (around 105-115°F or 40-46°C) is essential for activating the yeast, ensuring your dough rises beautifully.
*   **Yeast:** Active dry yeast or instant yeast can be used. Instant yeast can often be mixed directly with the dry ingredients, while active dry yeast benefits from being proofed in warm water first.
*   **Salt:** Fine sea salt is incorporated into the dough for flavor, while coarse sea salt is sprinkled generously over the top before baking, providing those delightful salty crystals that contrast beautifully with the rich olive oil.
*   **Extra Virgin Olive Oil:** This is non-negotiable. A high-quality, fruity extra virgin olive oil is the soul of Focaccia Genovese. It contributes significantly to the flavor, moisture, and crispness of the crust. Don't skimp here – the better the oil, the better your focaccia.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** Sprigs of fresh rosemary, finely chopped, are scattered over the dough. Its piney, slightly peppery aroma and flavor are the perfect complement to the olive oil and salt, creating that classic Mediterranean essence.

## Step-by-Step: Making Focaccia Genovese

While the process of making Focaccia Genovese requires a little patience, it’s surprisingly straightforward and immensely rewarding.

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a large bowl, combine the lukewarm water with your yeast. If using active dry yeast, let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy, indicating it’s active.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Begin mixing with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms. Drizzle in a good amount of extra virgin olive oil (around 2-3 tablespoons) and continue to mix until the dough comes together. It will be quite sticky – resist the urge to add too much extra flour.
3.  **Knead (or Fold):** Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for about 8-10 minutes until it becomes smooth and elastic. Alternatively, for a no-knead approach, perform a series of "stretch and folds" in the bowl over a period of an hour or two.
4.  **First Rise:** Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl, turn it once to coat, cover with plastic wrap or a damp cloth, and let it rise in a warm place for 1-2 hours, or until it has doubled in size.
5.  **Prepare the Pan:** Generously coat a large baking sheet (approximately 9x13 inches or larger for a thinner focaccia) with extra virgin olive oil. Don't be shy; this oil contributes to the crispy bottom crust.
6.  **Shape and Dimple:** Gently transfer the risen dough to the prepared baking sheet. Using your oiled fingertips, gently stretch and press the dough to fit the pan, being careful not to deflate it too much. Once spread, create the signature dimples by pressing your fingers firmly into the dough, almost touching the bottom of the pan.
7.  **Second Rise & Topping:** Drizzle more extra virgin olive oil generously over the dimpled surface. Sprinkle with the chopped fresh rosemary and coarse sea salt. Cover the pan loosely and let the Focaccia Genovese rise again for another 30-45 minutes.
8.  **Bake:** Preheat your oven to a high temperature (around 400-425°F or 200-220°C). Bake for 20-30 minutes, or until the focaccia is golden brown on top and beautifully crisp on the bottom.
9.  **Cool & Serve:** Once out of the oven, transfer the focaccia to a wire rack to cool slightly before slicing and serving.

## Tips for Perfect Focaccia Genovese

Achieving that perfect, airy, and flavorful Focaccia Genovese is easier with a few key pointers:

*   **Embrace Hydration:** Focaccia dough is typically very wet and sticky. This high hydration is what contributes to its airy texture and open crumb. Avoid adding too much flour during kneading or shaping. Oiled hands are your friend!
*   **Quality Olive Oil is King:** We cannot stress this enough. The flavor of your extra virgin olive oil will profoundly impact the final taste of your focaccia. Invest in a good bottle.
*   **Patience with Proofing:** Don't rush the rising times. A slow, steady rise allows the yeast to develop more flavor and contributes to a better texture. The dough should look visibly puffy and light.
*   **Generous Dimpling:** The dimples are crucial. They create pockets for the olive oil to collect and infuse, leading to those incredibly flavorful, crispy bits on the surface. Don't be afraid to press firmly.
*   **Hot Oven, Quick Bake:** Focaccia bakes best in a hot oven. This helps create a rapid rise (oven spring) and a beautifully crisp crust without drying out the interior.

## Serving Suggestions for Your Focaccia

The beauty of rosemary olive oil Focaccia Genovese lies in its incredible versatility. It can elevate almost any meal or stand proudly on its own.

*   **As an Appetizer:** Serve it alongside a platter of cured meats, cheeses, olives, and a bowl of high-quality balsamic vinegar for dipping.
*   **With Soups and Salads:** Its hearty yet light texture makes it the perfect accompaniment to a warm bowl of minestrone or a fresh caprese salad.
*   **Sandwiches:** Slice the focaccia horizontally and fill it with your favorite Italian deli meats, cheeses, roasted vegetables, or a simple smear of pesto for an extraordinary sandwich.
*   **Breakfast or Snack:** Enjoy a slice plain, perhaps with a drizzle of honey, or even dipped in coffee, Italian style, for a delightful morning treat.
*   **Pesto's Best Friend:** The subtle flavors of the focaccia make it an ideal vehicle for scooping up a vibrant Ligurian pesto.

## Focaccia Variations & Substitutions

While the classic rosemary olive oil Focaccia Genovese is perfect in its simplicity, home bakers often enjoy experimenting with variations.

*   **Other Toppings:** Before the final proof, consider adding sliced cherry tomatoes, thinly sliced onions, black olives, or even a sprinkle of Parmesan cheese. A dash of dried oregano can also enhance the Mediterranean flavor profile.
*   **Different Herbs:** While rosemary is iconic, fresh thyme or a mix of Italian herbs can also be used.
*   **Sweet Focaccia:** While less common for the Genovese style, some regions of Italy feature sweet focaccia, often topped with grapes (focaccia all'uva) or a dusting of sugar after baking.
*   **Flour Blends:** For a slightly nuttier flavor and denser texture, you could experiment with a small percentage of whole wheat flour blended with your bread flour, though this deviates from the traditional light Genovese crumb.
*   **Garlic Infusion:** Minced garlic can be added to the olive oil drizzle before the final proof for a more pungent flavor.

Remember, while variations are fun, the true essence of Focaccia Genovese shines brightest when its core ingredients are of the highest quality and handled with care.

Baking Focaccia Genovese is more than just following a recipe; it's an experience that connects you to generations of Italian culinary tradition. The aroma that fills your kitchen, the satisfying texture, and the rich, savory taste of this rosemary olive oil flatbread will transport you straight to the sun-drenched hills of Liguria. Whether served as a humble snack or the star of a grand meal, this iconic Italian baking delight promises to be a delicious and rewarding endeavor. So, roll up your sleeves, gather your finest ingredients, and prepare to bring the authentic taste of Italy into your home.
